docs.title: The `QEMU` holospace real QEMU, on the substrate, in the tab
docs.summary: The holospace real QEMU, on the substrate, in the tab is a holospace that runs real, unmodified QEMU as a κ addressed Wasm code module over the hologram host ABI (the ADR 008
docs.body: The holospace real QEMU, on the substrate, in the tab is a holospace that runs real, unmodified QEMU as a κ addressed Wasm code module over the hologram host ABI (the ADR 008 execution surface, ), booted by the holospaces engine itself ( in the browser). It is not the Emscripten/ browser build: that binds a browser/WASI host surface and runs beside the engine, which ADR 008/009 refuse (Law L1, ). This QEMU runs on the holospace native instance , one emulation layer, content addressed end to end. QEMU here is a workload , never the engine. QEMU as the substrate is rejected (ADR 009: off the shelf emulators bind WASI/a browser host). QEMU as a κ addressed userland is "authoring a program" (ADR 004/008) no spec change, no new law. See the engine's note, which this realizes directly on the engine instead of nested in a guest. Why this and not the other two ways Way One sentence Verdict QEMU nested in the guest ( ) your wasm core → Debian → QEMU TCG → guest works today, two emulation layers, slow the engine's example Emscripten in the tab QEMU bound to Emscripten's browser host, beside the engine fast to ship, but bypasses the substrate not "on the instance", not QEMU as a κ module (this) QEMU → wasm32 over the hologram host ABI , run by the engine conformant, one layer, content addressed the chosen path How it maps to the architecture (conformance) Piece What it is Spec / ADR · witness QEMU itself compiled to wasm32 binding only the hologram host ABI; gated by before it may be a holospace's code ADR 008 · The guest a real x86 64 PC under QEMU (SeaBIOS/OVMF, q35/i440fx, PCI) TCG, no KVM QEMU · oracle The disk the guest image as κ addressed content (a backed block device), sparse zero + deduped ADR 011 · The console QEMU / over the holospace console channel → xterm.js, delta streamed (O(new)) ADR 010 · Networking QEMU user net (slirp) → the userspace TCP/IP NAT → a WebSocket egress relay ADR 014 · Warm start the running QEMU+guest as a κ snapshot resume is O(1), not a cold boot / Identity the QEMU module, the firmware, and the disk are content (κ), not locations; verified by re derivation Laws L1/L5 The terminal is the same full xterm.js 5.5.0 stack as ( , provenance pinned, Law L5) core + WebGL + fit + web links + search + unicode11 + clipboard. Nothing about the terminal is QEMU specific; the console is just another holospace console channel ( ). What "make the most of UOR + O(1)" means here UOR storage the QEMU module, OVMF/SeaBIOS, and the guest disk are κ blobs. Identical sectors dedupe; the whole holospace is one κ that re derives byte for byte on any peer (Laws L1/L2/L5). The disk is the κ disk (sparse zeros). UOR networking guest egress is content on a channel, tunnelled out the relay; no raw socket in the tab. O(1) compute boot. Second and later launches resume from a κ snapshot ( / ) instead of cold booting QEMU + the guest O(1) in the snapshot, not O(boot). This is reused from verbatim. O(1) compute translation (staged, the headline win). Stock QEMU throws its TCG translation block cache away on exit. Phase Q4b content addresses each block by κ = H(guest bytes + cpu flags): a block translated once on any peer is reused on every peer, forever "re executing identical inputs rebinds rather than recomputes; a graph level memo hit is O(1)" (hologram). QEMU is then slow only the first time the network ever sees a code path. This is the genuinely novel piece and is staged , not in v1. Files the holospace: the xterm.js terminal + thin chrome (name · κ · status · save/reboot), the COI shim, the boot overlay, wired to the worker. the workload worker: fetches the κ addressed QEMU module + guest disk, validates the module against the host ABI surface ( ), boots it through the engine's interactive userland Workspace, streams the console delta to xterm and feeds keystrokes back, and warm resumes from a κ snapshot when one is present. the artifact recipe (CI / devcontainer, not Windows): builds to wasm32 over the hologram host ABI and stages the x86 64 guest disk. Follows QEMU's own build + system emulation docs (<https://gitlab.com/qemu project/qemu/ /tree/master/docs ). + → the CC 6 ABI conformance guest : a 2.5 KB, zero import Wasm module implementing the mailbox ABI (the executable contract; not QEMU). Boot it through the real driver today: . It proves the whole pipeline boot, live console, input echo, and O(1) κ snapshot suspend/resume. the conformance witness (spec ): drives the fixture in a headless browser and asserts boot + interactive console + O(1) resume with state continuity. Witnessed live. ( is the separate byte differential for real QEMU once its module is built.) + + → + a real minimal x86 CPU core as a CC 6 guest (a real ISA interpreter the same kind of thing as QEMU's TCG, ~2.4 KB, zero import) that executes a real x86 boot sector and forwards COM1 to the console. Boot it: . NOT QEMU (it decodes only the handful of opcodes a serial boot sector needs), but it is real machine code, really emulated, in the browser. Witnessed byte exact by (spec ). Build the artifacts (CI / devcontainer) Requires the hologram host ABI sysroot (a libc whose syscalls bind the host module, not WASI the ADR 008 "POSIX/libc layer compiles to such a module") and a wasm32 clang. The script exits loudly if that sysroot is absent it is the substantial port piece, not a download. See Staged work below. Run Without the artifacts the holospace loads, shows the terminal chrome, and reports "QEMU module not built yet" with the build command the honest state. Conformance phases (no false green a phase is only when its witness passes) Composes existing engine rows; the QEMU specific witness is (spec id ), driven byte against the oracle the same differential authority / already use. Phase Claim Composes Status Q0 the interactive driver (boot/run/console/feed/suspend/resume) + pattern witnessed live , via the CC 6 fixture Q0b a real x86 boot sector (real machine code) executes byte exact through the pipeline + a minimal x86 ISA core witnessed live Q1 the QEMU wasm32 module is a valid userland (imports only the host ABI, presents the container ABI) , target Q2 QEMU boots the x86 64 guest to a serial marker, byte identical to the oracle + / pattern target Q3 interactive console + κ disk + tunnelled networking + + driver built; witness pending the module Q4a warm resume from a κ snapshot (O(1) boot) / driver built; witness pending the module Q4b content addressed translation block cache (O(1) translation reuse across peers) the UOR memoization law staged (research) Status & remaining work (the honest fault line) Done the interactive userland Workspace. (crates/holospaces web/src/lib.rs) is a real / / / / surface that drives a validated CC 6 module on the same interpreter the engine uses, with a linear memory mailbox for the live console. Built in tree in the product crate (kept in hologram os per directive 2026 06 08, not pushed upstream); it compiles and is clippy clean for and is exported by the peer ( ). It is proven end to end : the CC 6 fixture ( ) boots through it with a live console, input echo, and O(1) κ snapshot suspend/resume with full state continuity , all checks green. With the real QEMU module still absent, the default page reports the honest "QEMU module not built yet." This resolved the earlier open question: no /substrate change was needed. The browser wires an empty (no host imports) and a one shot lifecycle, so deliberately drives directly the container ABI is the module's own exports + a linear memory mailbox, and the snapshot is a linear memory dump. A valid CC 6 module must export all five (the container ABI check; may be a stub) plus and . The exact contract is in the struct's doc comment + the fixture; must satisfy it. Remaining: 1. The QEMU module + the host ABI sysroot. Compile to wasm32 that (a) imports nothing (a self contained CC 6 module, so accepts it), (b) exports the mailbox ABI ,

docs.title: Holo Messenger deploy & use
docs.summary: Holo Messenger deploy & use A content addressed, end to end encrypted team chat (Mattermost style: channels, DMs, threads, reactions, @mentions, files, presence) that runs entire
docs.body: Holo Messenger deploy & use A content addressed, end to end encrypted team chat (Mattermost style: channels, DMs, threads, reactions, @mentions, files, presence) that runs entirely as a static site GitHub Pages serves the page + wasm; there is no server you run. How it works (so the constraints make sense) App: + + a prebuilt wasm bundle ( ). All static perfect for GitHub Pages. Transport (rendezvous): a chat needs a place for peers to meet. Pages can't host that (it's static), so by default the deployed app uses a public MQTT over WebSocket broker ( ) zero infra. The broker is content blind: it only ever sees ciphertext on unguessable topics . On the app instead uses your local relay ( ). Invites & security: a workspace's secret lives in the URL fragment ( ), which browsers never send to any server. Every channel key derives from it, so without the invite link a workspace is unguessable and undecryptable. The link also carries the relay URL, so teammates need nothing but the link. Deploy to GitHub Pages 1. Build the wasm locally and commit it (CI can't the web crate uses local hologram paths): 2. Push to . In the repo: Settings → Pages → Source: GitHub Actions . The workflow publishes the app. For the app is at: https://hologram technologies.github.io/holospaces ilya/ (the workflow makes the site root , so that URL is the running messenger). Zero install the invite is the app There is nothing to download. The invite link is the Pages URL plus the workspace secret in the fragment, e.g. . A teammate clicks it → the browser fetches the static page + wasm → the browser becomes the peer and the messenger is running. No accounts, no app, no server to join. That is the holospaces model: content addressed compute that runs in the tab. Use it with your team 1. Open the Pages URL. A new private workspace is created and the address bar becomes your invite link. 2. Click ✎ to set your name; click Invite to copy the link. 3. Send the link to teammates. They open it → they're in the same workspace (same channels, same keys) → set a name → chat. Channels, DMs, threads, reactions, @mentions and (small) file attachments all work, end to end encrypted. Honest limits of the public broker mode No offline history: a public broker is live pub/sub with no durable store, so messages sent while you're offline aren't replayed. (Your own past messages persist locally in the browser.) Small files only: brokers cap message size (~256 KB). Shared/public: fine for a team test. For production, run your own broker or relay and put its URL in the invite ( or ), or upgrade the transport to a free serverless relay (e.g. Cloudflare Workers) for durable store and forward. The deeper security layer (MLS / RFC 9420 forward secrecy + post compromise security) is built and proven; it slots in behind this same UI when you want it.

docs.title: Adding a spec, standard, or bundle
docs.summary: Adding a spec, standard, or bundle How to add an external standard (or a whole task oriented bundle of them) to the conformance system, so it is easy to manage and impossible to dr
docs.body: Adding a spec, standard, or bundle How to add an external standard (or a whole task oriented bundle of them) to the conformance system, so it is easy to manage and impossible to drift. This is the contributor recipe for the gate in over . The model One catalog ( ), one gate ( ), flat witnesses ( ), two doc anchors ( ADRs, conformance catalog). The unit you repeat is one spec row + its witness + its ADR + its CC row . Nothing else is invented no per concern folders, no second repo. A second repo would break coverage : the gate only stays honest because it sees every shipped artifact at once. A bundle is just data: a named set of rows, from any categories, that together define a capability ("a compliant, performant, interoperable app"). A row joins a bundle with a field (string or array). The gate keeps bundles honest; the dashboard reads their readiness. The five invariants the gate enforces 1. One catalog, one gate. Never fork; coverage depends on it. 2. Pinned + witnessed against an external authority. Never self reference. You re derive conformance (Law L5), you don't assert it. This is what "compliant" means. 3. Coverage fails fast. A new in scope artifact unclaimed by a spec turns the gate red drift is structurally impossible, not policed after the fact. 4. link to for suites, so the docs stay authoritative. 5. Report only → strict ramp. Land a witness that SKIPs (exit 0, ) so it never false passes and never blocks; flip it to real checks when ready. Adopt a big bundle without breaking the tree on day one. Recipe one standard (or one bundle), one PR Scaffold the two machine readable pieces, then fill in the manual three: The scaffolder writes the pinned row and a witness skeleton that SKIPs until you implement it (so it never false passes). It then prints the three manual steps: 1. Implement re derive conformance against the external authority. Prefer pure static (green locally and in CI); if it needs an oracle (browser / emulator / network), SKIP when absent. Emit only on pass. 2. Wire the one gate add to . 3. Record the decision an ADR section in (why this standard, non goals) and a row in (invariant · external authority · enforcement · witness). Pin imported artifacts in , κ verified on import. Verify: . How bundles are kept honest §1b (runs in , no oracle needed): every a row references must be defined in ( + + external ) a typo fails the build; every defined bundle must be non empty . Readiness is derived , never hand set: the full gate writes per bundle into under . A bundle is only when every member is witnessed. How it surfaces GitHub the CI job runs the structural gate as a blocking check; (the conformance catalog) renders on the Pages docs site; (with ) is uploaded as a build artifact. Add a README badge per bundle if you want at a glance status. Hologram UX the Specs section reads (grouped by ) and the pills from . Bundles are a second grouping: render as cards, each showing and a READY state the same data, grouped by capability instead of by category.

docs.title: Holo Stream OBS Studio, hologram-native
docs.summary: Holo Stream OBS Studio, hologram native Holo Stream is OBS Studio realized as a holospace: live screen + camera streaming and recording of an isolated holospace environment, enti
docs.body: Holo Stream OBS Studio, hologram native Holo Stream is OBS Studio realized as a holospace: live screen + camera streaming and recording of an isolated holospace environment, entirely in the browser , with every recording and every live room content addressed. No install, no media server. What it is A standalone holospace ( ) over a pure engine ( ) and a page UI ( ). It reads like every sibling holospace: a thin chrome bar (identity · · Law L5 · REC/LIVE status), the self manage affordance, the theme, the teleport share, the mobile layer, and a handle. It is reached from the Hub, the boot menu, the fleet, and a per frame broadcast icon docked on every holospace to the right of the Holo Capture icon ( , loaded by ). Faithful to OBS (its model → what we reuse) OBS's real model is Scenes → Sources → Audio Mixer → Record → Stream . Each maps to a browser standard or an existing Hologram engine an orchestration layer, not new infra: OBS Holo Stream Display / Window / Browser source (the "Holospace" source captures this tab) Video Capture Device / Audio Input (camera / mic) Scene + scene item transforms a program canvas compositor (normalized transforms, PiP/Side/Grid) → Audio Mixer (per source mute/volume) Web Audio + a gain node per source Recording (→ file) → bytes → κ = sha256 → OPFS κ store (the file IS its content address) Streaming (output) (a) serverless WebRTC mesh ( → a room) + (b) WHIP (OBS 30's protocol) ids each source kind carries OBS's id ( , , , , …) WHIP is honored byte faithfully (+ ) → + + answer SDP → the session resource. Reuse, not reinvention Serverless egress reuses Hologram Meet's mesh ( ) via one tiny added shim, (replaces the published senders with the composited program no new transport). Sources/record/compose are the W3C capture/record/canvas standards. Audio mixes with Web Audio; the mic can be enhanced via . The five laws 1. Content, not location a recording is ; a live room is a . 2. Canonical forms the scene collection ( ) canonicalizes → one κ. 3. The store is memory recordings live deduplicated by κ in the OPFS κ store. 4. Everything through the substrate the mesh is serverless; signalling is AES GCM sealed, content addressed κ pub/sub on a content blind relay; media is DTLS SRTP peer to peer. 5. Verify by re derivation the engine re derives a recording's κ; a stored clip re derives to its filename κ on read. On every track change the player re derives the playing bytes' sha256 and shows ✓ κ verified in the chrome a forge fails the match and is refused. The chrome κ address is . Use it Open . The thin top chrome shows the identity, the playing track's κ address, the Law L5 ✓, the transport state, a skin picker (all 13 skins), ⛶ fullscreen ( ), and ⤴ share (a teleport link). On mobile it just runs. On a phone (coarse pointer / narrow viewport) the fixed 275 px Winamp UI is scaled up to fill the device width ( , recomputed on resize/orientation), the stage scrolls, and the chrome wraps with ≥44 px touch targets; pinch zoom stays enabled. The skin picker is a native , so switching skins is one tap. Witnessed on a 390×844 phone viewport (boots · κ verified · fits · plays). Its UOR superpower: IPFS IS content addressing, so the holospaces Law L5 is native every block's multihash is re-derived against its CID right here in your browser (the IETF Trustless Gateway contract). The gateway is never trusted; a tampered byte is refused. Open any CID, ipfs:// / ipns:// link or DNSLink site, walk UnixFS directories like folders, preview files inline, discover who hosts a CID, drop a file to compute its real CID with no daemon, and pin-to-own into the content-addressed κ-store offline-ready and holo://-addressable. The same bytes carry a CID, a did:holo and a holo:// alias on both the sha2-256 and BLAKE3 axes. Open CIDs, ipfs:// / ipns:// links and DNSLink sites in one omnibox. Every block re-derived against its CID in-browser Trustless Gateway, Law L5. Multi-gateway racing for low latency; the first VERIFIED block wins. Walk UnixFS directories, preview images/media/text/PDF, discover providers. Add files locally for a real CID with no daemon; pin-to-own into the κ-store

A prism miner runs real proof-of-work too honestly browser-native, since no browser can out-hash mainnet's ASICs. Real self-custodial wallet keys on-device, send/receive real BTC. Full opcode set, gas schedule, precompiles & hardfork rules (EthereumJS reference). Content-addressed state: a Merkle-Patricia κ-store keyed by keccak256; the state root is a κ-label; code is addressed by codeHash. Run · Deploy · Call with an opcode-level trace (pc · op · gas · stack), storage, memory & logs. Live Ethereum mainnet view: every block hash re-derived (RLP→keccak) & κ-verified (Law L5)

It mines, sends and explores REAL BRC on the live public network, with no install, no extension and no simulation. Proof-of-work is real Argon2id (32 MiB) via our κ-pinned prism-argon2id engine byte-identical to BrowserCoin's own verifier, so the blocks it grinds are network-valid and every block is validated by BrowserCoin's real consensus (ASERT retarget, account model, Ed25519). The wallet has a real scannable QR, real on-device transaction signing and broadcast, and a live activity feed. Boot resumes from a content-addressed state snapshot, so it's on the live tip in seconds. Full BrowserCoin UX: Home · Wallet · Mine · Explorer · Mempool · About · Developers · Settings. Real Argon2id PoW (κ-pinned engine), real consensus, real mainnet over content-addressed REST. Real wallet scannable QR, Ed25519 send/receive, live balance + activity. Fast boot from a content-addressed snapshot; every block validated locally (Law L5), no simulation

docs.title: Holo EVM (a real, spec-faithful Ethereum Virtual Machine in the browser · EthereumJS reference engine, passes ethereum/tests · executes genuine EVM bytecode + signed transactions with full gas metering and an opcode-level trace · UOR content-addressed state: a Merkle-Patricia κ-store keyed by keccak256, the state root is a κ-label, code is addressed by codeHash · live mainnet view, every block's 16-field header re-derived RLP→keccak and κ-verified against the network · κ-FORK: a trustless, light-client-grade fork of mainnet execute real deployed contracts locally over state Merkle-PROVEN against a re-derived block root (eth_getProof verified per touched account/slot, code verified by codeHash), trusting no RPC; composes with Holo Scan via a ?fork deep-link + holo-evm-fork postMessage · κ-WITNESS: mint a portable, content-addressed witness (κ = keccak256 of canonical bytes) of a mainnet computation the re-derived header + exactly the Merkle proofs + the verified code that ANYONE re-executes in the real EVM fully OFFLINE (network physically disabled), re-deriving the header, re-rooting every proof and reproducing the result, trusting no node and touching no network; a flipped byte breaks the κ and the proofs (stateless verification / The Verge, made portable) · FULL-BLOCK VERIFIER: load a self-contained EF ethereum/tests consensus vector (complete pre-state + RLP block + expected post root), re-execute the whole block in the real EVM and recompute the Merkle-Patricia post-state root committed in the header the exact check a validating node performs, offline, no network; the same engine that runs your contracts passes the Ethereum consensus suite (full-block tier of The Verge) · Law L5)
